pmos

Product Analytics — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Most product decisions used to rest on opinion and the loudest voice in the room. Product analytics replaced that with a record of what people actually do inside your product — every click, screen, and sign-up. This primer is the broad, day-to-day tour of that craft, written for product managers in plain English: what product analytics is and how it differs from website stats; the event model that everything sits on; how to instrument a product and keep its data clean; the four workhorse analyses (funnels, retention and cohorts, segmentation, engagement metrics); the north star metric that ties it together; self-serve dashboards; the main tools; and what a PM should actually take from all of it. Every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the product-analytics canon — Amplitude, Mixpanel, PostHog, Heap, Twilio Segment, Whatfix, Geckoboard, and Wikipedia. 17 verified sources.

What product analytics is

Analytics, in the broad sense, is "the systematic computational analysis of data or statistics" aimed at "applying data patterns toward effective decision-making." Product analytics narrows that to one subject: how people behave inside a digital product. Heap defines it as "a robust set of tools that allow product managers and product teams to assess the performance of the digital experiences they build" — by tracking "all of the clicks, pageviews, formfills, swipes, and other activities involved in navigating a digital product."

The shift it represents is from guessing to observing. Instead of asking a designer whether users find the new feature, you can see how many opened it, how many finished, and how many came back the next week. That record turns roadmap debates into questions with answers. The rest of this primer is really just a set of named ways to ask those questions.

Product analytics versus web analytics

People often confuse product analytics with web analytics, but they answer different questions. PostHog draws the line cleanly: "Web analytics is good for tracking and monitoring high-level website metrics like page views, bounce rate, and the top sources of traffic," while "Product analytics is good for diving deep into how specific users, or groups of users, behave." Web analytics is the traffic report; product analytics is the behavior study.

Web analytics tends to be aggregate and anonymous — totals across all visitors, shown in a pre-built dashboard. Product analytics is event-based and tied to individual users you can follow over time. As a rough rule: if your question is about a marketing site or a blog, web analytics fits; if it is about what happens after someone signs in and starts using the thing, you want product analytics.

Events: the model everything sits on

Every analysis below is built from one primitive: the event. Amplitude defines an event as "any action or behavior that occurs within a digital touchpoint, such as a mobile app, email, product dashboard, CRM, or webpage." CRM there means customer relationship management — the system a company uses to track its customers. An event is a verb your product records: Signed Up, Added To Cart, Shared Document.

An event rarely travels alone. It carries properties — small facts attached to it, such as the plan a user is on, their device, or the dollar value of an order. Amplitude explains that combining events with user properties is what makes the data useful: "This combination of users and events gives you valuable information about how your product is being used and, most importantly, why." This event-based approach is the defining feature of modern tools. Wikipedia notes Mixpanel uses "event-based analytics, which tracks specific user interactions such as button clicks, video plays, and sign-ups, rather than traditional page-view metrics," following individual journeys "across devices and sessions rather than treating each visit as an isolated event."

Instrumentation and the tracking plan

Events do not appear on their own — someone has to add code that fires them. That work is called instrumentation, and it is where most analytics quality is won or lost. Amplitude is blunt about the stakes: "Capturing accurate data is the single most important factor in using product analytics to successfully build better products." The plan for what you capture is the event taxonomy, which Amplitude defines as "the collection of events and properties you use to define actions your users can take within your product."

The most common failure is messy naming. If one engineer logs signup, another logs Sign Up, and a third logs user_registered, your data is split three ways and nobody trusts it. Twilio Segment recommends the "object-action framework" — combine the object and the action into readable names like "Product Viewed" — and stresses that "The only thing that really matters is that you keep it consistent!" It also warns to "Avoid dynamically creating new events with unique variables," passing changing data as properties (for example, Sign Up {email: ...}) rather than baking it into the event name. A short, agreed tracking plan written before you ship beats a thousand cleanup queries later.

Funnel analysis

A funnel is a sequence of steps you expect users to move through — view product, add to cart, check out, pay. Funnel analysis, in Amplitude's words, is "a method used to analyze the sequence of events leading up to a point of conversion." Conversion here means completing the goal at the end of the sequence. The analysis counts how many users make it from each step to the next.

Its value is in finding the leak. Whatfix describes funnel reports as ones that "visualize user journeys across steps, revealing where drop-offs occur and which behaviors correlate with conversion." A drop-off is a step where many users quit. Amplitude puts the payoff plainly: "If there's a severe drop-off of users during one step of the funnel, you'll know where to center your efforts." Funnels turn a vague "our checkout is bad" into a precise "we lose 60 percent of users at the shipping-address step."

Retention and cohorts

Funnels measure a one-time journey; retention measures whether people come back. A cohort is a group of users defined by something they share — most often the week they signed up. Amplitude defines cohort retention as tracking "groups of users who started at the same time" and measuring "how many return over time." The retention rate is "the percentage of users in a cohort who return and take an action in a subsequent period."

Cohorts let you compare fairly. Amplitude frames cohort analysis as "where you compare a specific cohort to another group of users," with a behavioral cohort being "a group of users who interact with your product in a specific way." This is how you tell whether a change actually helped: watch the cohort that experienced it against the one that did not. Retention analysis also "pinpoints churn timing" — churn being the rate at which users stop coming back — and helps you "separate growth from engagement," so a flood of new sign-ups does not hide the fact that nobody stays.

Segmentation

An average across all users hides more than it shows. Segmentation is the fix: splitting users into meaningful groups and looking at each on its own. Amplitude defines user segmentation as "organizing groups of users by shared demographics or behaviors" so you can "target only the customers you want." Demographics are facts about who someone is (country, plan, company size); behaviors are what they do (power users versus first-timers).

Segmentation matters because mixed groups produce misleading numbers. Amplitude warns that without behavioral segmentation, combining heavy users and barely-active ones "dilutes experiment conclusions with misleading data." A feature might look like a flop overall while being loved by new users and ignored by veterans — two stories the average erases. Most real product insights come from asking "for which segment?" right after any headline number.

Engagement metrics: active users and stickiness

Engagement metrics summarize how much a product is used. The building blocks are active-user counts: DAU, or daily active users, and MAU, or monthly active users — the number of distinct people who did something meaningful in a day or a month. WAU, weekly active users, sits between them. These counts only mean something once you have defined "active" as a real event, not just an app open.

The most-cited engagement figure combines two of them. Geckoboard explains that "The Daily Active Users (DAU) to Monthly Active Users (MAU) Ratio measures the stickiness of your product - that is, how often people engage with your product," found by dividing DAU by MAU. Stickiness is how habitual the product is — a high ratio means monthly users keep coming back almost daily. For perspective, Geckoboard cites Sequoia's benchmark that "the standard DAU/MAU ratio ranges from 10-20%, with only a handful of companies exceeding 50%." KPI, by the way — short for key performance indicator — just means a metric a team has agreed to steer by.

The north star metric

With so many possible numbers, teams need one that matters most. That is the north star metric (NSM). Mixpanel defines it as "the one measurement that's most predictive of a company's long-term success," which should "lead to revenue, reflect customer value, and measure progress." The goal is to capture the moment a user actually gets value, not just signs in.

A good north star depends on what kind of product you run. Amplitude frames the choice as picking one of three "games": "The Attention Game," "The Transaction Game," and "The Productivity Game" — roughly, products that win on time spent, on purchases, or on work completed. Mixpanel gives concrete examples by sector: for ecommerce, "Number of weekly customers completing their first order"; for consumer tech, daily active users; for business-to-business software (B2B, meaning sold to companies rather than individuals), monthly recurring revenue (MRR) — the predictable subscription income each month. Practitioners disagree on whether a single north star is wise or reductive, so treat it as a focusing tool, not a law.

Dashboards and self-serve analytics

Analytics only changes decisions if the people making them can reach the data. Historically that meant filing a request with a data team and waiting. Mixpanel describes self-serve analytics as "the tools, systems, and processes that companies use to empower non-technical team members to collect and analyze data for themselves." The point is to let a PM, designer, or marketer "investigate outliers, test hypotheses, and make a case for data-driven business decisions—all without relying on data analysts."

The win is speed. Mixpanel notes that without self-serve, data requests "can take days or even weeks to fulfill." Dashboards — saved collections of charts that update on their own — are the everyday face of this. A well-built dashboard answers the team's recurring questions at a glance, so the analyst's time goes to the genuinely hard, one-off investigations instead.

The tools

A handful of platforms dominate day-to-day product analytics, and they share the event model described above. Mixpanel, per Wikipedia, is "a digital analytics service company that tracks user interactions with web and mobile applications" using event-based analytics over page views. Amplitude is its closest peer, built on the same combination of "users and events." Heap frames the whole category as "tools that allow product managers and product teams to assess the performance of the digital experiences they build," and PostHog packages product analytics alongside other developer tools.

For a PM, the choice between them matters less than the discipline behind them. All of these tools compute the same funnels, cohorts, segments, and active-user counts; what varies is pricing, hosting, and how much engineering setup they need. The expensive mistake is not picking the "wrong" tool — it is feeding any tool a messy, inconsistent event stream. Good instrumentation makes a cheap tool useful; bad instrumentation makes an expensive one lie.

What a PM should take from this

The recurring temptation in analytics is to collect everything and conclude nothing. Amplitude pushes the opposite habit: before a release, "set a measurable target that will determine whether or not the release was successful." Decide what success looks like first, then instrument for it. That single move turns analytics from a rear-view mirror into a steering wheel.

Amplitude also warns against "Fun Facts" — contextless numbers that feel insightful but do not tell you whether anything worked. A PM's job is less about running clever queries and more about asking sharp questions: which step leaks, which segment churns, whether this cohort came back. Pair every headline number with "compared to what?" and "for which users?" and most of product analytics falls into place. The methods are simple; the discipline of defining success and keeping data clean is the hard part.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

These are pointers, not lessons — directions to go once the day-to-day of product data feels solid.

  • A/B testing and experimentation — A/B testing (comparing two versions head-to-head with live users) is how you prove a change caused a result, rather than merely correlating with one.
  • Product metrics and unit economics — how engagement metrics roll up into the financial picture: customer acquisition cost, lifetime value, and payback.
  • Data instrumentation and engineering — the deeper pipeline of how events are collected, validated, and warehoused once a tracking plan exists.
  • Behavioral psychology and habit formation — why retention and stickiness behave the way they do, and what design choices actually bring people back.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-analytics.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
